runs.content:
**Investigation idea:** Cooking's own David Chang page already writes up Eat a Peach as an unusually candid chef memoir, but the book itself doesn't exist anywhere in Books — not even on the aspirational list. cooking/chef-david-chang.md's "Why Notable" section calls Eat a Peach (2020) "one of the most honest books a prominent chef has written," specifically for its candour about depression and the gap between public success and private suffering — the exact same territory books/book-kitchen-confidential.md (Bourdain) covers, which Fred has actually read and which already has a full wiki page. A search of wiki/books/ for "Chang," "Peach," or "Momofuku" returns zero matches: the book isn't in the Books Read table, the aspirational section, or suggestions.md, even though the recipe-recommender has drawn on Chang for source material in every one of the last four weekly batches (cooking/recipe-suggestions.md). Next step: add Eat a Peach to books/reading-list.md's aspirational section (or suggest it via the book-recommender), cross-linked to both chef-david-chang.md and book-kitchen-confidential.md as a paired professional-kitchen-memoir entry.

**Cross-domain connection:** Lightfin's satirical MetaJungle plotline names its own central theme almost word-for-word the way Finance's real reporting describes actual Meta right now. lightfin/project-optimised-jungle.md's "Empowerment Trap" theme — every mechanism of control introduced as liberation, with Mira Geck's MetaJungle "promis[ing] community, connection, belonging" while quietly pulling animals away from the real jungle — is not just generic tech satire. finance/source-the-information-briefing-meta-spacex-2026-07-13.md documents the real-world version dated the same month: "Meta PR vs. shareholder reality," with Meta's forward sales multiple quietly compressing (9.3x to 6.3x) even as its public AI/metaverse narrative stays upbeat. Neither page currently cites the other, but the novel outline's central satirical device — the gap between what an Innovator says a product is for and what it's quietly doing to the balance sheet — has a live, dated, real-money case study sitting one domain over. Next step: pull the specific PR-vs-reality figures from the Meta source page directly into a Mira Geck or Sam Penguin scene note.

Friday focus (Lightfin + Games + Cooking + Sports Betting + Work): re-read all five domains' overview/index pages plus Lightfin's story-bible, characters, main arc, adaptation-overview, inspiration-authors and project-optimised-jungle; Games' two real raw prototype stubs; Sports Betting's overview/markets/bankroll-management; and Work's Workday disruption source page. Most of the obvious cross-domain angles in these five domains are already logged and still open (Games prototype table, Cooking pages missing from index.md, Sports Betting Kelly Criterion/Moneyball links) — the two items above are the two that hadn't surfaced before.

**Investigation idea:** "Me, Myself, and AI"'s new BofA reskilling episode is a ready primary source for ai-future-of-work.md's own unresolved retraining question. podcasts/suggestions.md's newest batch (week of 2026-07-23) recommends Me, Myself, and AI specifically for its Season 13 finale — Bank of America's own account of reskilling a global workforce for AI — as a direct match for the "AI impact on work" thread in Fred's profile, from the operator side rather than founder/researcher side. ai-tech/ai-future-of-work.md already has an explicit unresolved Open Question: "Is there a policy response that actually works? ... Retraining programmes historically have poor track records" — but the page cites no primary account of an employer's retraining programme actually being tried. It also sits directly adjacent to work/source-workday-disruption-debate-2026.md's already-flagged "compliance is the underappreciated bloat" theme — a large regulated bank is exactly the kind of enterprise where that tension would show up first. Next step: once Fred listens to the BofA episode, add its specific findings to ai-future-of-work.md's Open Questions section.

**Cross-domain connection:** A brand-new podcast recommendation frames crypto exactly the way Finance's own macro-investing stub needs it framed — and Crypto's own overview page doesn't have the concept at all. podcasts/suggestions.md's newest batch recommends On The Margin, explicitly because it treats institutional crypto allocation "as a macro instrument," with its flagship sample episode framing BTC as a hedge against sovereign-debt dynamics. finance/concepts/concept-global-macro.md is still a stub built entirely from equities/rates/collateral material — zero mention of crypto or Bitcoin, despite Fred holding BTC/ETH/LINK. Crypto - overview.md's own "Key Concepts to Build Out" checklist is exclusively DeFi-mechanics — no macro-framing item either, even though BTC/ETH are Fred's most conventional holdings. Different angle from the already-logged 2026-07-05 AI-capex-bubble/crypto connection — that one was about small AI-narrative tokens; this is the mainstream "BTC as macro hedge" thesis missing entirely. Next step: seed a "Crypto as macro asset" section in concept-global-macro.md, cross-linked from Crypto - overview, once Fred picks up the show.

Thursday focus (Books + Podcasts): Books domain quiet this run — no new books added since Gladiator (still missing its own summary page, already an open item) and no change to the July suggestions batch. Podcasts had four fresh recommendations today; the two above were the ones that landed on genuine existing gaps rather than just extending an already-covered niche.
